lesson: Design style 'Lopsided Static': (1) break mirror symmetry on paired parts using dx/dy/angle/scale so sides are never even; (2) give exactly one part (eyes or mouth) an oversized scale 1.4-2x as the focal point while others stay modest; (3) pair body tint and limb/detail tint from opposite sides of the color wheel for instant silhouette legibility; (4) tilt the whole body 5-15 degrees off vertical so the monster looks frozen off-balance.

When placing two overlapping eyes of very different scale (e.g. 0.8 and 1.8) close together (dx 25 vs 35), the smaller eye gets fully occluded by the larger one's sprite bounds - defeats the intended asymmetry since only one eye ends up visible. Need dx separation roughly proportional to the larger eye's scaled radius, or offset the smaller eye further outward/downward, so both remain visible.

A body angle tilt (5-15deg) is nearly invisible on round/oval body shapes (B, C, D) since they're rotationally symmetric-looking; the 'frozen off-balance' effect reads much better on angular or asymmetric body shapes (A, E, F) or must be reinforced by tilting an attached part (mouth/eyes) in the same direction to sell the lean.

add_arms/add_legs/add_antennas(count>=2) apply one angle/dx/dy/scale to the WHOLE mirrored pair, so both sides move identically (mirrored) - this produces a symmetric flex/V pose, not true left-right asymmetry, no matter how extreme the angle. For 'Lopsided Static' asymmetry, don't rely on arms/legs for lopsidedness; instead concentrate asymmetry budget on eyes (call add_eyes multiple times with different dx/dy/scale/style per call) and use antennas count:1 (a single antenna instantly reads as off-balance/asymmetric) or mouth dx offset.

Body shape E (long body) still reads as a fairly symmetric capsule/pill silhouette - a 10-12deg body angle is only faintly visible at the top edge. Shapes with inherent asymmetry (F - long body with hair tufts) or reinforcing the tilt with an off-axis single antenna/mouth in the same rotational direction sells the 'frozen off-balance' lean much more clearly than body angle alone.

Body shape F (long body with hair tufts) sells the 'frozen off-balance' tilt much better than plain capsule shapes B/C/D/E - the tufts visibly lean with the angle, giving a clear read of imbalance. Prefer shape F (or A) for this style over rounded/oval shapes.

Combining: one small secondary eye (scale ~0.6-0.7, subtle style like 'dead') + one oversized dominant eye (scale ~1.6-1.8, bold style like angry_blue) with dx separation proportional to the big eye's radius (~45-50px apart), plus a single antenna (count:1) and a mouth nudged (dx 15-20) in the same direction as the dominant eye, reliably produces strong, legible 'Lopsided Static' asymmetry without needing arm/leg asymmetry.

User feedback (3.5/5) on part placement defaults: legs should be positioned lower on the body (increase default dy or use larger positive dy offset), arms should sit slightly lower and closer to center (positive dy and negative dx-widening, i.e. reduce dx or use small negative dx to pull them in), and the oversized 'dominant eye' focal scale should be dialed back from ~1.6-1.8 to something more like 1.3-1.5 - the largest tested scale read as too big/dominant rather than striking.

Tool bug: add_arms/add_legs/add_antennas/add_eyes each REPLACE this.monster.arms/legs/antennas/eyes wholesale rather than accumulating - calling the same tool twice (e.g. two mirror:false calls to fake left+right asymmetry) leaves the first call's sprites orphaned (still visible, but untracked so clear_monster/future updates won't destroy them - memory leak). For left/right asymmetric limbs, ALWAYS use a SINGLE call with mirror left at default true) and differing colorLeft/colorRight, poseLeft/poseRight, angleLeft/angleRight, dxLeft/dxRight, scaleLeft/scaleRight etc. - this is fully supported and is the correct way to get true asymmetry without the overwrite bug.

add_arms/add_legs schema requires base 'color' and 'pose' even when colorLeft/colorRight and poseLeft/poseRight fully specify both sides independently - omitting the base fields throws 'missing required parameter: color'. Tool should make color/pose optional when both *Left and *Right overrides are provided, since the base value is otherwise unused dead weight.

add_eyes 'positions' array works well for asymmetric per-eye placement in one call (dx/dy/scale/style/tint/angle all overridable per index) and is preferable to calling add_eyes twice, but the 'dead' eye style renders as a small, dark, low-contrast X shape with no bright color of its own - on medium/dark tinted bodies it becomes nearly invisible unless given an explicit bright tint or scale boost above ~1x.

build_monster composite tool works well end-to-end for a full symmetric build in one call (body+arms+legs+eyes+mouth+antennas) and correctly clears any prior monster since create_body wipes the scene - good default entry point for straightforward, non-asymmetric monsters.

With count:3 eyes and dominant:true, the default spacing (40px) stays fixed regardless of the dominant eye's 1.4x scale-up, causing severe overlap between all 3 eyes (they visually merge into a blob) - dominant scaling should be paired with increased spacing (or spacing should scale with the largest eye's radius) when count>=3, otherwise use dominant only with count:2 or manually widen dx per-eye via positions.

Arm pose E at scale 1.2 is very large/wide and, combined with default arm offset, visually covers most of a shape-A body's silhouette and tint from the front view - oversized arm poses (D, E) at scale>=1.1 work better on bigger/wider body shapes (E, F) or should be paired with scale<=1.0 on compact bodies (A, B) to keep the body tint/color legible.

Non-uniform body scaleX/scaleY (e.g. 1.3/0.85) combined with a modest angle (-8deg) on shape D still barely shows the tilt - flattening the body actually makes rotation even less perceptible since the silhouette becomes more disc-like. For legible tilt with non-uniform body scale, either use a stronger angle (15+deg) or stick to uniform scale when tilt legibility matters.

A small secondary eye (scale 0.7) placed at dx30/dy20 on the same side as a low-angled asymmetric arm (angleRight -60, dyRight +20) gets fully occluded by the arm sprite extending across that region - when combining asymmetric arm rotation with small secondary eyes, keep eye dx/dy on the opposite side from any inward-swinging arm, or increase eye dy (move it higher, away from arm's swing path).

Tinting an antenna (base color green) with a contrasting hex (#ffee00 yellow) only visibly recolors the small tip/bulb of the detail_antenna sprite - the thin stalk part stays close to its original base color. For antennas, tint reads best as an accent on the tip rather than a full recolor; if a fully different antenna color is wanted, change the 'color' param (which swaps the whole texture) instead of relying on tint.

add_eyes with count:6 at default spacing (40px, hardcoded in parts.js, not exposed as a tool param) produces a total span of 200px+, wider than every body shape's silhouette - eyes visibly overflow past the body edges. Tool improvement needed: expose 'spacing' as an add_eyes parameter (like antennas already imply via evenly-spaced layout) so high eye counts can be packed tighter, or auto-shrink spacing based on count so the row stays within body bounds.

Body shape E (long pill/capsule) with a single asymmetric arm+leg (mirror:false) reads as a strange one-limbed creature that's legible and amusing, but the unused side looks empty/unbalanced - single-limb asymmetry works best paired with an offsetting element on the opposite side (e.g. an antenna cluster or eye offset) to keep the composition from feeling like a mistake rather than a deliberate design.

Applying tint:'#ffffff' (pure white) to any part is a no-op visually - Phaser's setTint multiplies texture color by the tint, and multiplying by white (255,255,255) leaves the original color unchanged. To lighten/wash out a part's color, a tint alone won't work; white tint should not be used expecting a color change.

User feedback (3.5/5, repeated across two review rounds): default arm placement (PARTS.arm.offset y:10) reads as floating/disconnected from the body and sits too high - should increase default dy further (try +30-40 more than current) so arms visually attach near the body's side/shoulder rather than hovering near the top.

User feedback (3.5/5): default leg placement (PARTS.leg.offset y:100) is still too high on most body shapes/sizes - legs should sit lower, closer to the visual bottom edge of the body silhouette. Recommend increasing default leg offset.y substantially (e.g. to ~130-150) or scaling leg dy relative to body scale/shape height, since fixed offset doesn't account for body size variation.

User feedback (3.5/5): with higher eye counts (4+), eyes stack horizontally using fixed 40px spacing and visibly extend past the body's silhouette edges - looks bad. Needed tool fix: either auto-reduce spacing as count increases (spacing = min(40, bodyWidth/count)), cap effective row width to stay within body bounds, or wrap eyes into multiple rows for count>4 instead of one long horizontal line. Until fixed, avoid count>3 eyes unless manually compensating with tighter dx via positions overrides.

Fixed in parts.js/scene.js: arm default offset.y raised from 10 to 45 and leg default offset.y raised from 100 to 140 to stop arms/legs from floating too high/disconnected from the body (user feedback, 2 review rounds). Also added PARTS.eye.maxRowWidth (140px) and made add_eyes auto-shrink spacing (min of default 40px and maxRowWidth/(count-1)) so eye rows no longer overflow past the body silhouette at count>=4. Browser tab must be reloaded to pick up these scene.js/parts.js changes since it's plain script tags with no hot-reload.

Confirmed fix works: with arm offset.y=45 and leg offset.y=140, both limbs now visually attach to the body (shoulder/hip) instead of floating - big improvement over prior defaults. Remaining nitpick: the raised leg dy pushes legs low enough that on shape F they now sit almost fully below the body silhouette with a visible gap, so for shape F specifically consider dy in the 120-130 range rather than the new 140 default, or pair with stance:'high' to compensate.

On a body scaled up 1.3x (large silhouette), the fixed absolute eye offset/spacing (not scaled with body) makes two dominant-scaled eyes sit close enough to visibly overlap (one eye's white sclera edge cuts into the other) - eye spacing should ideally scale with body.scale, or manually increase dx spread when body scale>1.2 to avoid overlap. Also confirms body scale 1.3 + leg offset.y=140 pairs well, no gap this time (round shape B doesn't taper like F).

Negative dx on add_arms (e.g. dx:-20 applied to mirrored pair) successfully pulls both arms inward toward the body center without them overlapping the torso awkwardly - good technique for a more compact/hugging arm stance, complements the earlier note that positive dx widens the pair. Also: paired antennas (count 2) with a shared angle (e.g. -25) both tilt the same direction since they're mirrored around center, producing a 'windswept' look rather than true asymmetry - for asymmetric antenna lean, use count:1 or manually add two separate calls is not possible (overwrite bug, lesson 9), so single antenna remains the only clean way to get one-sided antenna asymmetry.

Style brief: An organic, feral creature style built around asymmetry read as anatomy, not decoration — mismatched limbs should suggest old injuries that regrew wrong, not a stylistic flourish. Palette stays muted and fleshy: lean on dark and red bases, keep tints close to their base hue rather than washed-out or pastel, and reserve any bright/saturated tint for occasional accents that read as inflammation or infection, not as decoration. Eyes are never a symmetric, centered pair — uneven size and off-center placement is the rule, not the exception. The unsettling quality should come from things being subtly anatomically "wrong" (an extra antenna reading as a growth, a mismatched leg stance reading as a limp) rather than from gore or shock — restraint is what makes it creepy instead of cartoonish.

Limb coloring baseline (gallery/wound-bloom/001.png vs 002.png): give an asymmetric limb the SAME base color as the body (usually dark), tinted close to its own hue with only a small luminance offset (~10-15 below body luminance). A whole limb tinted a saturated color (e.g. full red arm+leg in 001) reads as a bolted-on prosthetic, not an old injury — reserve strong saturation for small accent zones only.

Eye asymmetry legibility (gallery/wound-bloom/001.png, 003.png): both eyes must stay individually readable as eyes even when uneven. A huge bright/saturated eye (e.g. angry_red at large scale) reads aggressive/cartoonish; a soft or closed style shrunk below ~0.9 scale (e.g. closed_happy at 0.65) nearly vanishes and reads as a cyclops instead of an uneven pair. Prefer higher-contrast styles (human, human_red, dead) kept in a moderate uneven range (~0.7-1.15 scale) so asymmetry reads as anatomy, not as one-eye-missing or one-eye-shouting.

Growth/accent visibility (gallery/wound-bloom/002.png, 003.png): small growths (antennas, etc.) need luminance contrast against BOTH the body AND the dark navy background, not just the body. A dark-base tint on a growth (luminance ~20) nearly disappears into the background. Using a red base with a muted (not fully saturated) tint gives a growth luminance ~55-65 — visible as a subtle 'infection' accent without looking decorative.

Pose choice (gallery/wound-bloom/003.png): bent/limb-like arm poses (B/C) sell 'regrew wrong' anatomy better than decorative/hooked poses (e.g. D's big loop), which read as stylized rather than injured.

Scale asymmetry cap (EXPERIMENT, gallery/wound-bloom/004.png): limb scale differences should stay within roughly 0.7x-1.5x of each other. Pushing to 2.3x made the oversized limb dominate the whole silhouette, flipping the read from 'subtle old injury' to 'dramatic monster-boss feature' — a violation of the brief's restraint principle. Confirmed as a hard upper bound going forward.

gallery/wound-bloom/005.png: body shape F (hair tufts) reads well for 'feral/organic' — the small ragged spikes around the silhouette add texture without extra tooling, and its irregular edge helps sell asymmetry even before limbs are touched. Good default body shape for this style over plain A/B/C.

gallery/wound-bloom/005.png: the antenna growth sits far above the head, disconnected from the body silhouette — it reads as a floating decorative antenna rather than an attached growth/wart. Pull growth placement's dy closer to the body outline (less negative dy, or overlapping the head/shoulder) so it reads as emerging FROM the anatomy rather than hovering above it.

gallery/wound-bloom/006.png: nudging the antenna growth's dy to +45 (down into the head/shoulder region instead of floating above) makes it read as an attached wart-like growth breaking the silhouette, matching the brief's 'extra antenna reading as a growth' goal — much better than 005's floating antenna. Confirmed fix for growth placement.

gallery/wound-bloom/006.png: the 'dead' eye (small X) at 0.75 scale paired with a full-size 'human' eye at 1.1 scale reads as clearly uneven AND both are individually legible (X vs round white sclera) — better asymmetric eye pairing than 003's shrunk closed_happy. Adopt dead+human (or dead+human_red) as a reliable go-to uneven-eye combo.

EXPERIMENT (iteration 8, gallery/wound-bloom/007.png): deliberately made both eyes identical (same style/scale/symmetric dx/dy), violating the 'never symmetric' rule while keeping every other element (mismatched limbs, growth, muted palette) unchanged from 006. Result: the monster instantly reads as a normal, friendly, symmetric-faced cartoon character despite the asymmetric limbs still being present — the limb asymmetry alone isn't enough to sell 'wrongness' once the face is symmetric. Confirms eye asymmetry is the single most load-bearing rule in the brief for the unsettling read; it can't be sacrificed even when other asymmetry cues are present elsewhere on the body.

gallery/wound-bloom/008.png: placing the small 'dead' eye at dy+12 (below the natural eye-line, near the mouth) makes it overlap the mouth's teeth detail — the two small light-colored shapes (eye X and teeth) blur together into one confusing cluster instead of reading as a distinct small eye near a normal mouth. Keep off-eye-line dy offsets moderate (~+/-8 max) so a shrunk eye stays visually separated from the mouth.

gallery/wound-bloom/009.png: built and exercised two experimental capabilities — add_scar (procedural Graphics jagged line) and jitter_part (one-time random dx/dy/angle nudge on an existing part). The scar drawn just under the left eye (dark red-brown #5a1c14, length 55) reads convincingly as an old wound without any gore/shock — restrained and anatomical, exactly per brief. jitter_part on the dark leg (small +7/+7/+7 nudge) added a subtle 'off' quality on top of the existing pose/tint asymmetry, reinforcing the limp read without being a big visible change by itself — best used as a finishing touch on top of deliberate asymmetry, not as the asymmetry's main driver.

gallery/wound-bloom/009.png: editing scene.js to add experimental commands triggers a live-reload that wipes the current monster (this.monster resets to {}) — must rebuild_monster after every scene.js edit before testing experimental_command calls, or they'll fail with 'no body exists yet' / 'no X exists' even though the previous build succeeded moments earlier.
